text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 19 March 2020 

Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist,  

Jonathan Patel, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH982114 NHS No. 8426113795  
Flat 12, 29 Wren Street, London WC1X 0HB

Thank you for your referral and for facilitating remote reviews given his travel schedule. I conducted a scheduled telephone consultation today while he was in transit between time zones for work. He reports long-standing focal epilepsy, with events that typically begin with a rising epigastric sensation and a brief pause in speech, followed by right-hand automatisms and retained partial awareness; occasionally these progress to impaired awareness with brief confusion afterwards. He denies generalized tonic–clonic activity. Seizure days: eight/30 this month, which he links to frequent red-eye flights, irregular sleep, and skipped meals across time zones. He keeps a digital seizure diary that correlates increased events with weeks involving multiple intercontinental flights.

He reports reliable auras (epigastric rising and déjà vu) lasting 10–20 seconds, sometimes allowing him to sit or excuse himself before automatisms develop. Post-event recovery is usually under 10 minutes, with mild fatigue and word-finding difficulty. No injuries have been reported. He does not drive. Medication adherence is generally good but he admits to occasionally missing the second daily dose during long-haul travel; he has since set phone reminders aligned to local time.

He has focal epilepsy, most consistent with a temporal lobe onset based on semiology and prior records he summarised over the phone (he reports a previous MRI as structurally normal and an outpatient EEG with left temporal sharp waves, though we have not yet verified these results). He has tolerated his current regimen without adverse effects beyond mild somnolence on sleep-deprived days. He has not used rescue medication to date. He denies mood change, psychosis, or prolonged post-ictal confusion. No features suggestive of dissociative events were described.

Given the clear association between travel-related sleep disruption and seizure occurrence, he is motivated to continue structured self-management strategies (sleep scheduling on flights, hydration, regular meals, and consistent dosing reminders). He is open to an in-person review when his schedule allows, but prefers telephone appointments in the interim; he can usually accommodate early UK morning slots before flights. We agreed to obtain previous imaging and EEG reports to confirm the suspected left temporal focus and to consider ambulatory EEG if events remain frequent despite improved adherence across time zones.

I will update you once external records are received and after his next telephone review in eight weeks. Please let me know if you would like any additional information in the meantime.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: 'Seizure days: eight/30 this month.' This provides a clear numeric frequency within a monthly period. Interpreting 'eight/30 this month' as 8 seizure days per 1 month. No other competing frequencies are provided that are higher. Therefore, normalize to '8 per month.'
